item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: seconding (nth-ing?) a lot of comments on here - i feel the most promising thing here is the piecing together of the mystery from future copies of the same researcher, and eventually becoming them, but then he just gets sucked into a hole and goes "yep, space's haunted"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is maybe the closest Ive gotten to "that dialogue seems a bit too prose-y for me" with the end but also it makes sense someone who's seen every reality would talk like that.

Either way I have a new love for space SCP and this one delivers (and has made me learn about a cool real life thing as well)

item.title: The Peregrine-9 Exploration Crew
item.content:encoded: Hey did anyone notice that the potrait the crew took that is one of the Soyuz Flight to the ISS?Soyuz TMA-19M Crew

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good point, and I've edited it accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, will change now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bogoroditsa. footnote: "Mother of God" in Russian.

Technically true. However, this is pretty much an "official" name, used in prayers and the like. If you're going for an awed exclamation or something like that, you can use "Mater' Bozhya" instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"I saw all of it. All of the times we tried to stop it in the past. All the times that I tried to stop it in the past. And the future. This happens again and again, until we get it right.

Over and over again, we try and try to fix it. All times blend together until we reach singularity and all is lost. The tear opens more holes everywhere in reality, and the anomalies appear faster and faster, but containment is only delaying the inevitable. The inevitable cleansing of the slate."

This has gotta be related to Dr. Mackenzie's proposal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If its border was 700MLY away at the time of 700M years BP, and its expansion rate is 10MLY/year, then it should reached our current position (700M-70) years ago.

i.e. for all objects that are moving faster than light, we will only see it after they actually passed us.

In other word the light cone effect https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light_cone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I like the SCP itself, temporal sinks are a bit too OP for for my tastes. As of now there's nothing stopping them from being attached to research sites to solve problems instantly, or attaching one to an anti-grav battleship to create an invincible killing machine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Late response, but the Bootes void is roughly 700 million lightyears away, so the anomaly hasn't quite reached us yet. Of course, any pictures we take of it are also 700 million years in the past, hence the need for a more up close and personal investigation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Forgive me if I'm mistaken here, but…

"The anomaly is expanding at a rate of 10 million light years per year."

Then how do we have a civilian photo of it? Wouldn't it reach Earth before the light for the picture does?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I am, that makes a lot more sense. In any case this is definitely and upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand this line from the final log: "The void isn't a region where spacetime is ruined; it's the source of the tear itself."

If the void isn't the tear, then what is the tear then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're making the all-too-common mistake of mistaking Chronos for Cronus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this one nicknamed Chronos? I enjoyed this one very much, but fail to see why it was called that. It doesn't seem to have anything with the former king of the gods who attempted to maintain his rule by devouring his children, only to be eventually overthrown by his children and imprisoned in tartaris.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is true

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff Notice: This user has been permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much this sentiment exactly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt the need to communicate my feelings but have found a sudden void (Boötes Void?) of words. Still, the feeling won't go away unless I say something.

This brings nothing new to the table. Something sucking up the universe, different timelines, parallel universes, and a guy who suddenly has an epiphany and delivers to us a creepy monologue. It's just… meh.

I think the most interesting thing about this is your description of how they travel through space. So, -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, thank you for correcting this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this, and not just because it has a form of FTL travel I've never seen before.

Temporal Sinks are headcanoned so hard right now.

EDIT: Maybe this is a bit of a selfish plug, but I wrote SCP-3029, and based it partly on the tech used in this skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. It describes a concept that is anomalous in every way, but allows a way for the reader to understand the nature of this entity. It also tells of an event that cannot be stopped, and effectively gives the reader a sense of helplessness
Take my upvote, good sir/madam

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noticed that the article still referenced "SCP-3000" in text, so I went and changed all of those to "SCP-3200", which shooouuuld be the correct number now, given the page number?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writing is very good, and I liked the worldbuilding and the way the timeline bleed-through presented itself, but agreeing with an earlier comment that it stops before really getting interesting.

The final reveal killed the story for me as-is; the trick of "this spooky anomaly is destroying the universe and we can see into other timelines and know we'll never be able to stop it!" is cliche. It really has been done so, so many times, including in an entry to the 2000 contest (which I didn't like, but still did that trope better). It's come up repeatedly elsewhere, too. And this version of it is so straightforward.

It's boring and I don't believe it.

At least, it doesn't work for me. This SCP is well over 100 in rating, so it's clearly working for a lot of people. Take this FWIW!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes Chaos Mage that's the one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks MrWrong lol, not sure how I accomplished that…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It sounds like this tale to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this space expedition tale about SCP-184.

Ahem… it is linking to www.wikidot.com, ahem…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't want to burst any bubbles, but I found this beyond cliché and I'm pretty surprised with how intrigued many people seem to be with it. Most of the clichés and questionable FTL methods have already been pinpointed in other comments, but more so I have to say this felt a little too similar to this space expedition tale about SCP-184. Don't get me wrong, that tale has some faults as well, but the overall gist of Chronos felt really underwhelming compared to it (once you connect it to the concept of 184). I'd say that if Chronos can become more intriguing than that, the title of SCP-3000 is well-deserved.

Otherwise, I do like the idea of the Boötes Void being an SCP, but a tear between universes is pretty unsatisfying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry but I'm really not following how time sinks allow for convenient interstellar space flight. If you speed up time on the ship relative to earth such that they can travel millions of light-years in a few months, wouldn't that result in a few months passing for the crew while millions (possibly trillions depending on their speed) of years pass on Earth? The crew might survive the journey but no one on earth is ever going to hear back from them again. Did I completely misunderstand the explanation?

item.title: Re: Author Overview
item.content:encoded:
Image sources: https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Boovoid.png

Second one is from Expedition 46 to the ISS. I can't find a compliant source though (it's almost certainly compliant as a public domain image, but I can't be sure of that until I find the original verson).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am ultimately disappointed by this one.

If the Boötes void was expanding at 10 million light years per year, it would already have swallowed our galaxy (like 630 million years ago), having expanded by 7×1015 light years from the size it was 700 million years ago that we see today. This also is a causality headache, obviously.

The use of time sinks as an FTL drive that doesn't violate causality doesn't really work, since we are still getting information from the future here. Also if they aren't also using some kind of at least relativistic stardrive, they are still arriving trillions or more (subjective) years in the future and are likely not even in the stellar era any longer.

The ending, after quite a lot of buildup and some excellent tension, just fizzles. So it's yet another cosmic threat that ineffably hates us. Get in line buddy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thaumiel is not the equivalent of uber-Keter. It is a class mostly for SCPs that are used by the Foundation as the metaphorical box to contain anomalies and protect normal people.

Besides, it takes more than a theme to declare the nuance of any article. A story about time need not be always horror; it can be romance, fantasy, comedy etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this should be Thaumiel sir because its about "time" and we all know time is the scariest sci-fi shit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure if this is substantial critique, but I'll put this out there: it's cliche. The isolation, the apocalyptic log from another universe, the "We Cannot Stop It, oh no" sentiment, the cutesy variation on the "gazing into the abyss" line. All of these parts just coalesce into a very generic, by the numbers SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a good concept, but I think it should be Apollyon. However, that is up for you to decide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might found my new fav. SCP. Hope this gets the 3000 SCP title :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with Deci and Ty. The writing is superb and sets up a scary premise - and that's it.

The premise is very promising though, so I'm not downvoting, because it genuinely piqued my interest and I liked a lot of things in this - but it has no satisfying end to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You played eight into Kitchen Sink without ever reading it. Looks like you understand the picture I've been painting pretty well. Kudos.

+1 for good use of temporal anomalies. I loves me some temporal anomalies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite like its portrayal of FTL, very original. The payoff was a little weak to me perhaps. I was really expecting this to be a false vacuum metastability event, where the universe is rewriting it self due to the Higgs crashing to a true ground state. Wait, has anyone wrote a skip on this before?

hmmm….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An excellent scip, although I feel like its more of an 001 sort of scp than a 3000 kind of one for some reason. Nice job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic. +1

We can't get any good data on it either because we still can't get any of our counters are working.

This is awkward, but it could be intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding. I finish feeling like not much of an impression has been made, despite the promising premise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Given that this is a horror contest entry, I ought to judge it by how scary it is. Unfortunately, weizhong, I'm not spooked! It's an 'empty' region of space where time is all wobbley and grouchy.

And this piece made me realise (but it's by no means unique to this article) the overusage of 'feeling off' this contest. Many articles include some variation of that phrase, and I feel that it's a little cheap. But it's not wholly fair to fault you for what others are doing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat.

I like the build up of the mystery and tension as the Peregrine crew travels deeper into the unknown, and the absolute terror that is encountered when they find exactly what they were looking for. An answer.

+1. Spooky stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has been a pretty common critique, and I understand that immensely. I initially had a much bigger draft (twice as long) but I cut down on it sharply because it felt bloated. I'm heavily considering rewriting the ending after the contest is over and maybe introducing more of the elements that I originally had.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You stopped right when your story started to pick up. It sincerely felt like you were about to dive so much deeper into what was going on, but it just ended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a wonderful space-skip.

I upvoted for your method of FTL travel alone - the rest is just icing on the cake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think this is a contest-winner, by far, but it is a good space skip. I am upvoting now entirely for this line

We have seen into the abyss, and by God, it hates us.

which is a ballsy fucking thing to throw into an article meant to evoke horror. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmmm… I didn't know this going in, but there's something about the words "Hume levels" that just suck all the horror right out of me. I was hooked at the initial description, but then… eh.

It was a pretty entertaining read, but I don't think the note at the end really had much punch or told us anything interesting about the void. There's good horror to be had in the central story and I liked at lot of the events described, but "empty featureless Hume-fluctuation inter-dimensional universe-destroying nexus/tear" is really boring as an antagonist/anomaly, and the note was just kind of melodramatic and sudden.

Edit: Much like "dangerous" or "powerful" as traits aren't inherently interesting, neither is "inevitable."

No vote. I should love this, but it's not quite there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Out of all the entries so far, this has to be my favorite one. Great job! +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Boötes Void

About time someone tackles that. And it was done super-well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah! Thank you for pointing this out. Adjusted accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I hate to break it to you, but that name's been taken since 2005.
Quick, call that space program something other than Falcon before you embarrass yourself in front of all the other space nerds that will inevitably read this page!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish that this had done a better job delivering the payoff. A Note at the End recorded 600 million light years away is still a Note at the End.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As Westrin has said, dread is something this definitely builds up — though not exactly what I'd call my idea of 'horror'.

But,
This was a very interesting read from start to finish. The real hook here was the discovery of that first box, and the events that followed it — events that lead me to question what the hell is the eventuality of this going to be?
This was incredibly well-done both in terms of it's concept and execution, and I'll happily give it the +1 it deserves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
over and over and over again

>:|

That being said I like the dread and atmosphere it built up, definitely one of my personal favorites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like I said in chat, this is a good example of ramping dread, and it does a nice payoff. +1'd to the face.
